response:
I don’t have access to your meeting notes or internal decisions, so I can’t state “what was decided” in your org. If you point me to the meeting/date or paste the decision text, I can interpret it. 

Absent that, here’s a concise, practical summary of the typical decision organizations make about SOX and AI development, plus the concrete controls and next steps that usually follow.

Typical decision options organizations choose
- Option A — Apply SOX to production systems only: development and test environments follow secure SDLC practices (code review, dev access controls, CI/CD gating) but only systems that affect financial reporting (or SOX-controlled processes) are brought under formal SOX change-control, evidence, and sign-off requirements before production release.
- Option B — Apply SOX controls to all environments that can materially affect production: more conservative — applies strong access, logging, and change controls to dev/test as well as prod.
- Option C — Hybrid: treat models/systems that can materially affect financial statements as SOX in all lifecycle phases; everything else follows lighter governance.

Common organizational choice
- Most companies adopt Option A or the Hybrid: require full SOX change-control and auditability for anything that affects financial reporting, while enforcing a secure SDLC and documented processes in non-SOX environments.

If your org decided to treat AI dev as SOX-relevant, key requirements and controls
- Scope & inventory: identify AI models, data pipelines and code that can affect financial reporting and list them in the SOX inventory.
- Segregation of environments: enforce separate dev/test/UAT/production with controlled promotion paths.
- Change management: formal change requests, approvals (including Finance/IT/ Audit owners where appropriate), scheduled releases, rollback plans.
- Access control & SOD: least privilege for prod access; separation of duties between developers and production deployers/approvers.
- Code and model review: peer reviews, automated static analysis, model validation results recorded.
- Testing & validation evidence: unit/integration tests, model performance and stability tests, backtesting, bias checks if relevant — retain test artifacts as audit evidence.
- Deployment controls: gated CI/CD, change tickets linked to deployments, release runbooks, signed-off deployments.
- Logging & audit trails: immutable logs for changes to code, model parameters, training data versions and production predictions where they impact controls.
- Data governance & lineage: record data sources, transformations, retention policies, masking for PII and financial data where applicable.
- Model governance: versioning, model cards, owner assignment, intended use, confidence/limits, monitoring plan.
- Monitoring & alerting: production monitoring for performance drift, concept drift, and exceptions with alerting and incident-response procedures.
- Evidence retention: store approvals, test artifacts, logs and model versions in an auditable repository for required retention periods.
- Third-party/cloud: confirm vendor/cloud contracts support audit evidence, encryption, access controls and SOC reports.

If your org decided NOT to treat AI dev as SOX-relevant (but still cares about controls)
- Document the rationale (materiality, impact analysis).
- Still implement secure SDLC and change-control for production deployments.
- Ensure proven controls for any indirect impacts on financial reporting.

Suggested immediate next steps to operationalize the decision
1. Confirm and capture the decision in policy and meeting minutes. Identify owner (CRO/CISO/Head of AI/Finance).
2. Perform quick materiality assessment / model inventory to identify what’s in scope.
3. Map existing SDLC and deployment processes to SOX control requirements and identify gaps.
4. Implement prioritized controls: environment segregation, change-control workflow, access controls, logging.
5. Set up an evidence repository and a routine audit schedule with Internal Audit/Finance.
6. Train developers, release engineers, and business owners on new controls.

If you want, tell me:
- The exact wording of the decision you heard, or
- Which AI systems you’re concerned about (models that touch revenue recognition, trading, forecasts, etc.),
and I’ll translate it into the specific controls, checklist and evidence package you’ll need for SOX audit.
